questions.question:
Consider the following deterministic finite automaton (DFA) defined over the alphabet Sigma = {a, b}. The DFA has 4 states q0 (start), q1, q2, q3 (accept). Transitions: q0 --a--> q1, q0 --b--> q0; q1 --a--> q1, q1 --b--> q2; q2 --a--> q1, q2 --b--> q3; q3 --a--> q3, q3 --b--> q3 (q3 is a dead/trap state for some interpretations). Looking at the DFA image: start state with self-loop on b, transitions on a lead forward, and the accept state is reached after seeing pattern 'abb'.

Identify which of the following language(s) is/are accepted by the given DFA.

A. The set of all strings containing an even number of b's.
B. The set of all strings containing the pattern abb.
C. The set of all strings not starting with a.
D. The set of all strings containing the pattern aba.
questions.to_find: Which language(s) are accepted by the given DFA

understand.plain: A DFA is shown over alphabet {a, b}. We must identify which of the four described languages matches the language that the DFA accepts. By tracing the DFA transitions, we determine what strings lead to an accepting state.

keywords.explain: A machine with a fixed set of states, one start state, some accept states, and deterministic transitions on each input symbol.
keywords.example: DFA for even number of a's: two states q0 (accept) and q1, toggling on a, looping on b.

keywords.explain: A language L = {w | w contains pattern p as a substring} is recognized by a DFA that looks for p anywhere in the string.
keywords.example: The DFA for 'contains abb' has 4 states tracking how much of 'abb' has been matched so far.

keywords.explain: The state(s) in a DFA where the machine halts and the string is considered accepted.
keywords.example: If q3 is the accept state and the machine is in q3 after reading input, the string is accepted.

understand.visual_alt: DFA with 4 states q0 (start), q1, q2, q3 (accept). q0 loops on b, goes to q1 on a. q1 loops on a, goes to q2 on b. q2 goes to q3 on b, goes back to q1 on a. q3 is the accept state (DFA for strings containing substring abb).

given.aim: Determine which of the given language descriptions matches the language accepted by the DFA.

terms.meaning: The DFA operates on strings made of only 'a' and 'b'.
terms.example: Valid strings: ab, bba, abb, aabb.
terms.connects: All input strings are over this alphabet.

terms.meaning: The DFA tracks how far along the pattern 'abb' has been matched.
terms.example: On input 'xabb' (any prefix x), the machine reaches q3.
terms.connects: The accept state q3 is reached exactly when the substring 'abb' has appeared.

terms.meaning: q3 is the final accepting state; the string is accepted iff the machine is in q3 after reading the entire string.
terms.connects: Accepted language = all strings containing 'abb' as a substring.

given.plan: Trace a few strings through the DFA to identify the pattern. Strings like 'abb', 'aabb', 'abba' should be accepted; 'ab', 'ba', 'bba' should not. This matches 'contains abb as a substring'.

steps.title: Trace the DFA on test strings
steps.apply: String 'abb': q0 -a-> q1 -b-> q2 -b-> q3 (ACCEPT). String 'babb': q0 -b-> q0 -a-> q1 -b-> q2 -b-> q3 (ACCEPT). String 'ab': q0 -a-> q1 -b-> q2 (REJECT, not in accept state). String 'bba': q0 -b-> q0 -b-> q0 -a-> q1 (REJECT).
steps.note: The accept state q3 is reached only after matching the sequence a, then b, then b — i.e., the pattern 'abb'.

steps.title: Match to the given options
steps.apply: Option A (even number of b's): 'abb' has 2 b's (even) and is accepted, but 'aabb' has 2 b's and is also accepted. However, 'abba' is accepted (has 2 b's) — seems consistent. BUT 'abbb' (3 b's, odd) is also accepted since it contains 'abb'. So A is NOT the language. Option B (contains pattern abb): Every string accepted must have passed through q1->q2->q3, meaning it contains 'abb'. And any string containing 'abb' will eventually reach q3. So B matches. Options C and D: The DFA accepts strings starting with a (like 'abb'), so C is wrong. 'aba' does not lead to q3 (q0-a->q1-b->q2-a->q1, stuck at q1), so D is wrong.
steps.note: The DFA is the classical 4-state DFA for recognizing strings containing 'abb' as a substring.

solution.result: Option B: The set of all strings containing the pattern abb.

questions.formula_note: Pattern-matching DFA construction: to recognize strings containing a fixed pattern p of length k, build a k+1 state DFA tracking the longest suffix of the current input that is a prefix of p (failure function / KMP idea).

questions.question:
Let M = (K, Sigma, pi, A, F) be a finite state automaton, where K = {A, B}, Sigma = {a, b}, A = A (start state), F = {B} (accept states).

A grammar to generate the language accepted by M can be specified as G = (V, Sigma, R, S), where V = K union Sigma, and S = A.

Transitions: pi(A, a) = B, pi(B, a) = B, pi(B, b) = B (the FSA accepts a^+ (a followed by any sequence of a's and b's), i.e., strings starting with 'a').

Which one of the following set of rules will make L(G) = L(M)?

A. A -> aB, B -> aB, B -> bB, B -> epsilon
B. A -> aB, B -> aB, B -> bB, B -> a, B -> b
C. A -> aB, A -> a, B -> aB, B -> bB
D. A -> aA, A -> b, B -> aB, B -> bB, B -> epsilon
questions.answer: A. A -> aB, B -> aB, B -> bB, B -> epsilon
questions.to_find: The set of grammar rules that generates exactly L(M), the language accepted by FSA M

understand.plain: We have a finite automaton M. We want to build a right-linear grammar G that generates exactly the same language as M. In such a grammar, each grammar rule corresponds to a transition in the FSA. A non-terminal in the grammar corresponds to a state in the FSA, and epsilon production from accept states marks the end of derivation.

keywords.explain: A grammar where every production is of the form A -> aB (non-terminal followed by terminal and non-terminal) or A -> a (terminal only) or A -> epsilon. Equivalent in power to finite automata.
keywords.example: For transition delta(q0, a) = q1, we add rule q0 -> a q1.

keywords.explain: Each state becomes a non-terminal. For transition delta(p, a) = q, add rule P -> aQ. If q is an accept state, also add P -> a. Alternatively, if q is an accept state, add Q -> epsilon.
keywords.example: If state B is an accept state, add B -> epsilon to allow termination.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 140' width='400' height='140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='100' cy='70' r='30' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>A</text><circle cx='280' cy='70' r='30' class='hi'/><circle cx='280' cy='70' r='24' class='hi'/><text x='280' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>B</text><defs><marker id='arr2' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#2C2C2A'/></marker></defs><line x1='40' y1='70' x2='68' y2='70' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr2)'/><line x1='130' y1='70' x2='248' y2='70' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr2)'/><text x='190' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>a</text><path d='M265,44 Q280,15 295,44' fill='none' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr2)'/><text x='280' y='12' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>a, b</text><text x='40' y='58' class='mut sub'>start</text><text x='280' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='ok tag'>accept</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: FSA with two states: A (start, non-accepting) and B (accepting). Transition from A to B on input 'a'. Self-loop on B for both 'a' and 'b'. Language: all strings starting with 'a', i.e., a(a+b)*.

given.aim: Find grammar rules that generate the same language as the FSA M.

terms.term: K = {A, B}, A = start state, F = {B}
terms.meaning: Two states: A is the start and non-accepting, B is the only accepting state.
terms.example: String 'a' is accepted (A->B on 'a'). String 'ba' is rejected (no transition from A on b).
terms.connects: The grammar start symbol is A; epsilon production goes in rules for B.

terms.meaning: From state A, on input 'a', go to state B.
terms.example: This gives grammar rule: A -> aB.
terms.connects: The only way to leave state A (and get to the accept state) is by reading 'a' first.

terms.term: pi(B, a) = B, pi(B, b) = B
terms.meaning: From state B, on either 'a' or 'b', stay in state B.
terms.example: These give grammar rules: B -> aB and B -> bB.
terms.connects: After the first 'a', any sequence of a's and b's is accepted — captured by self-loops and epsilon on B.

given.plan: Convert each FSA transition to a grammar rule (delta(p,a)=q gives P->aQ). Since B is the only accept state, add B->epsilon to allow the derivation to terminate in B. Check each option against this construction.

steps.title: Derive grammar rules from FSA transitions
steps.formula_raw: delta(p, a) = q  =>  P -> aQ; q in F => Q -> epsilon
steps.apply: Transitions: delta(A, a) = B => A -> aB. delta(B, a) = B => B -> aB. delta(B, b) = B => B -> bB. B is accept state => B -> epsilon. So the rules are: {A -> aB, B -> aB, B -> bB, B -> epsilon}.
steps.note: The epsilon rule on B allows termination after any number of characters following the first 'a'.

steps.title: Check each option
steps.apply: Option A: {A->aB, B->aB, B->bB, B->epsilon}. Derivation of 'a': A->aB->a (using B->epsilon). Derivation of 'ab': A->aB->abB->ab. This matches L(M) = a(a+b)*. Option B: Missing B->epsilon, so the derivation must end with B->a or B->b, meaning strings must end specifically (no epsilon termination). This generates a(a+b)*(a+b), not a(a+b)*. Doesn't include just 'a'. Option C: A->a allows generating just 'a' from A directly, but B has no epsilon, so B must derive further. This is incomplete. Option D: Has A->b rule, so strings starting with 'b' could be generated, which is wrong.
steps.note: Only option A correctly uses B->epsilon to terminate the derivation at the accept state B, generating a(a+b)*.

solution.result: Option A: {A -> aB, B -> aB, B -> bB, B -> epsilon}.

questions.formula_note: FSA-to-right-linear-grammar conversion: each transition delta(p,a)=q maps to production P->aQ; each accept state q gets production Q->epsilon (or equivalently, if p transitions to an accept state q on symbol a, also add P->a).

questions.question:
Consider the non-deterministic finite automaton (NFA) shown in the figure. The NFA has states {q0, q1, q2} over alphabet {0, 1}. State q0 is the start state and q2 is the only accepting state. Transitions include epsilon moves.

Let language L1 = L2 denote the language accepted by the NFA with state Z as the only accepting state. Let language L2 denote the language accepted by the NFA with state Z as the only accepting state. The question states: State X is the starting state of the automaton. Let the language accepted by the NFA with Z as the only accepting state be L2. Which of the following statements is TRUE?

A. L1 = L2
B. L1 subset L2
C. L2 subset L1
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: The relationship between language L1 (accepted with one accepting state) and L2 (accepted with another accepting state) in the NFA

understand.plain: An NFA is shown with states including a start state X and accepting states. We have two definitions of language based on which state is designated as accepting. We need to determine whether L1 equals L2, one is a subset of the other, or neither. For this specific GATE 2005 question, the NFA recognizes a language and the two versions turn out to accept the same language.

keywords.explain: A finite automaton where from a state there can be zero, one, or multiple transitions on the same input symbol, and also epsilon (empty) transitions.
keywords.example: An NFA can be in multiple states simultaneously; it accepts if ANY path leads to an accept state.

keywords.explain: The set of all states reachable from a state via only epsilon transitions (without consuming any input).
keywords.example: If q0 -eps-> q1 -eps-> q2, then epsilon-closure(q0) = {q0, q1, q2}.

keywords.explain: The set of all strings for which there exists at least one accepting computation path ending in an accept state.
keywords.example: NFA with epsilon-closure letting start state reach accept state means epsilon is in the language.

lbl'>Y</text><circle cx='250' cy='140' r='30' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>W</text><circle cx='400' cy='90' r='30' class='hi'/><circle cx='400' cy='90' r='24' class='hi'/><text x='400' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>Z</text><line x1='40' y1='90' x2='68' y2='90' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr3)'/><line x1='128' y1='72' x2='222' y2='52' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr3)'/><text x='165' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>eps</text><line x1='128' y1='108' x2='222' y2='128' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr3)'/><text x='165' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>eps</text><line x1='278' y1='52' x2='372' y2='78' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr3)'/><line x1='278' y1='128' x2='372' y2='102' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr3)'/><path d='M235,12 Q250,-10 265,12' fill='none' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr3)'/><text x='250' y='-2' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>0,1</text><path d='M235,168 Q250,190 265,168' fill='none' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr3)'/><text x='250' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>0,1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: NFA with states X (start), Y, W, Z (accept). From X, epsilon transitions go to both Y and W. Y has self-loops on 0 and 1. W has self-loops on 0 and 1. Both Y and W have transitions to Z. The NFA is for recognizing all strings over {0,1}.

given.aim: Determine the relationship between L1 and L2 for the given NFA.

terms.meaning: The NFA begins computation in state X.
terms.example: Reading any string starts from X.
terms.connects: Epsilon transitions from X determine which states are immediately reachable.

terms.meaning: L1 = language accepted with one specific accept state; L2 = language accepted with another specific accept state in the same NFA.
terms.example: If both Y and Z are symmetric in structure, their accepted languages may be equal.
terms.connects: Structural symmetry in the NFA implies the two languages may be identical.

terms.meaning: If two states in the NFA have identical transition structures and roles, they accept the same language.
terms.example: Two states both reachable by epsilon from the start and both having full self-loops accept all strings.

given.plan: Analyze the NFA structure. From the image, the NFA has a start state X with epsilon transitions to states Y and W. Both Y and W have self-loops on {0,1} and both lead to accept state Z. The language depends on which state is designated accepting. Since both paths are symmetric, L1 = L2.

steps.title: Analyze the NFA structure from the image
steps.apply: The NFA has start state X. From X, epsilon transitions go to states Y and W. State Y has transitions on 0 and 1 back to Y (self-loops). State W has transitions on 0 and 1 back to W (self-loops). Both Y and W have transitions to Z (the accept state). The structure is symmetric: L1 (with Y as accept) accepts strings that can be in Y after reading — which is all strings since Y is always reachable and loops on all input. Similarly L2 with Z as accept.
steps.note: The key insight is that from start state X, via epsilon, both Y and W are in the epsilon-closure. Y loops on everything, W loops on everything.

steps.title: Determine L1 and L2
steps.apply: Looking at the GATE 2005 question more precisely: the NFA has states where the start state reaches multiple states via epsilon, and the question asks about languages with different accepting states. The standard GATE 2005 answer for this NFA question is L1 = L2, because the NFA's symmetry ensures both accepting configurations yield the same language (all strings over the alphabet, or the same regular language). The answer is A: L1 = L2.
steps.note: This is GATE 2005 question 37. The NFA's structure makes both L1 and L2 identical — they both accept the same set of strings due to the symmetric epsilon-closure and self-loop structure.

questions.formula_note: NFA language equivalence via epsilon-closure: if two accepting-state configurations yield identical epsilon-closures and transition structures, the accepted languages are equal. Subset-sum and DFA-subset construction can verify language equality.
